metadata.title: AgentOS (agentos.sh): Open Source TypeScript AI Agent Framework
metadata.description: AgentOS at agentos.sh is an open-source TypeScript AI agent framework. Build autonomous agents with cognitive memory, runtime tool generation, multi-agent orchestration, and 21 LLM providers. 85.6% on LongMemEval-S. Apache 2.0. By Frame.dev.
metadata.ogTitle: AgentOS (agentos.sh): Open Source TypeScript AI Agent Framework
metadata.ogDescription: AgentOS at agentos.sh: open-source TypeScript AI agent framework. Cognitive memory, runtime tool generation, multi-agent orchestration, voice pipelines, and 21 LLM providers. 85.6% on LongMemEval-S. Apache 2.0.
metadata.twitterTitle: AgentOS (agentos.sh): Open Source TypeScript AI Agent Framework
metadata.twitterDescription: AgentOS at agentos.sh: open-source TypeScript AI agent framework. Cognitive memory, runtime tool generation, multi-agent orchestration, and 21 LLM providers. 85.6% LongMemEval-S. Apache 2.0.

hero.title: Open-Source TypeScript AI Agent Runtime
hero.subtitle: Open-source TypeScript runtime for AI agents that remember, adapt, and spawn new tools. Agents write their own functions mid-task and the judge approves them; managers create specialists when the static roster does not cover the task. Cognitive memory grounded in neuroscience. 85.6% on LongMemEval-S. Apache 2.0.

technicalHighlights.title: Streaming-first runtime
technicalHighlights.detail: Token-level delivery across 37 channel adapters and guardrails.

technicalHighlights.title: Multi-agent orchestration
technicalHighlights.detail: Parallel agents with auditable routing, approvals, and budgets.

technicalHighlights.title: Multimodal RAG
technicalHighlights.detail: 7 vector backends with 4-tier auto-scaling (SQLite → HNSW → Postgres+pgvector → Qdrant), observational memory compression, 10 document loaders, and Ebbinghaus decay.

technicalHighlights.title: Self-hostable
technicalHighlights.detail: Deploy anywhere — your infrastructure, your data, your agents.

features.sectionSubtitle: Production-grade infrastructure for building, deploying, and scaling AI agents

multiAgent.title: Emergent Multi-Agent Coordination
multiAgent.description: Spawn researcher, analyst, critic, and executor personas from a single goal. Shared context, budgets, and approvals keep every branch in sync.

toolPacks.title: Tool & Guardrail Packs
toolPacks.description: Ship opinionated extensions for search, vision, code execution, or custom APIs with built-in permissions and retries.

language.title: Language & Translation
language.description: Auto-detect locale and tone per surface. Personas stay fluent across voice, chat, and docs with translation providers of your choice.

storage.title: Storage & Deployment
storage.description: 7 vector store backends with 4-tier auto-scaling (SQLite → HNSW → Postgres+pgvector → Qdrant). One-command migration between all backends. Docker auto-setup for Qdrant and Postgres.

workbench.title: Local-First Workbench
workbench.description: Prototype entire agencies in-browser with persisted SQL, timeline scrubbers, and a built-in marketplace of personas.
workbench.bullet2: Sync artifacts back to the cloud
workbench.bullet3: Export full agencies as Markdown/JSON bundles

guardrails.title: Guardrails & Safety
guardrails.description: Built-in content moderation, PII detection, and compliance checks. Circuit breakers, cost guards, and stuck detection prevent runaway loops and excessive spending.
guardrails.bullet1: Circuit breaker + cost guard + stuck detection

streaming.title: Streaming Runtime
streaming.description: Token-level streaming with real-time output. Built for low-latency interactions with support for SSE, WebSockets, and custom transports.

workflow.title: Workflow Engine
workflow.description: Define complex multi-step workflows with conditional branching, parallel execution, and automatic retry logic.

capabilityDiscovery.title: Smart Capability Discovery
capabilityDiscovery.description: Semantic, tiered discovery engine that reduces capability context by ~90%. Vector search + graph re-ranking finds the right tool, skill, or channel at the right time.

memoryCompression.title: Observational Memory Compression
memoryCompression.description: LLM-based 3-10x memory compression with temporal reasoning. Self-improving consolidation that merges, prunes, and strengthens memories over time.

documentIngestion.title: Document Ingestion Pipeline
documentIngestion.description: 10 document loaders with multimodal extraction. 3 PDF processing tiers (basic, OCR, vision). 4 chunking strategies (fixed, semantic, recursive, sliding window).

memoryPortability.title: Memory Import / Export
memoryPortability.description: Import and export agent memory in 10 formats including Obsidian vault, ChatGPT exports, Markdown, JSON, and CSV. One-command migration between all 7 vector backends.

marketplace.description: Share, buy, and sell your AgentOS agencies. We'll handle CI/CD, infrastructure, payouts, and compliance so you can focus on adaptive, emergent, permanent intelligence.

caseStudies.description: Real-world AgentOS implementations and production deployments

footer.tagline: Open-source TypeScript AI agent runtime by Frame.dev

channels.description: Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Twitter, and 32 more. Voice via 12 STT + 12 TTS providers. Every channel adapter streams tokens in real-time through a unified protocol.

personality.name: Personality (HEXACO)
personality.description: Honesty-Humility, Emotionality, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Openness. Traits modulate memory encoding, communication style, and risk tolerance.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about AgentOS — from first install to production deployment.

whatIsAgentOS.question: What is AgentOS?
whatIsAgentOS.answer: AgentOS is an open-source TypeScript runtime for building production AI agents, developed by Manic Agency LLC (manic.agency) and Frame.dev. It provides multi-agent orchestration, cognitive memory, multimodal RAG, built-in safety guardrails (PII redaction, prompt injection defense, content moderation), voice pipelines, 37 channel adapters, and 21 LLM provider integrations. AgentOS also powers the AI systems behind Wilds.ai, an AI-native gaming platform. Self-hostable and free.

isOpenSource.question: Is AgentOS free and open source?
isOpenSource.answer: Yes. The core runtime is licensed under Apache 2.0. Agent presets, extensions, and guardrails are MIT-licensed. You can use AgentOS commercially, modify it, and redistribute it under these license terms.

llmProviders.question: What LLM providers are supported?
llmProviders.answer: AgentOS supports 21 LLM providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Mistral, Cohere, Ollama, OpenRouter, Together AI, Fireworks AI, Groq, Perplexity, DeepSeek, xAI (Grok), Replicate, Anyscale, AI21 Labs, Aleph Alpha, AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, Cloudflare Workers AI, and HuggingFace Inference. Provider fallback chains allow automatic failover.

language.question: What programming language is AgentOS built in?
language.answer: AgentOS is written entirely in TypeScript and runs on Node.js. The npm package is @framers/agentos. It works with any TypeScript or JavaScript project.

comparison.question: How is AgentOS different from LangChain, CrewAI, or AutoGen?
comparison.answer: AgentOS is TypeScript-native (not Python), includes cognitive memory with Ebbinghaus decay curves and HEXACO personality modeling, offers 5 security tiers instead of basic prompt filtering, provides 37 built-in channel adapters (Discord, Telegram, Slack, email, and more), and supports 6 orchestration strategies including graph-based agent DAGs. It also ships with 88 curated skills and a capability discovery engine that reduces token usage by roughly 90%.

whatIsParacosm.question: What is Paracosm?
whatIsParacosm.answer: Paracosm (https://paracosm.agentos.sh) is a structured world-model simulation engine built on AgentOS. It compiles a JSON ScenarioPackage plus a HEXACO leader profile into a deterministic turn loop where AgentOS agents propose events, forge new TypeScript tools at runtime inside a hardened node:vm sandbox, and produce a counterfactual artifact you can fork and replay. Three modes share one schema: turn-loop civilization sims (Mars Genesis is the canonical example), batch-trajectory digital twins, and batch-point Monte-Carlo forecasts. Open-source under Apache 2.0, npm install paracosm. Live demo: https://paracosm.agentos.sh/sim. Source: https://github.com/framersai/paracosm. Long-form launch post (why we built it, the wilds.ai vault story, the engine reference): https://agentos.sh/blog/paracosm-launch.

whatIsWildsAi.question: What is Wilds.ai?
whatIsWildsAi.answer: Wilds.ai (https://wilds.ai) is an AI-native gaming platform built on AgentOS and Paracosm. AI characters with persistent cognitive memory and HEXACO personality traits inhabit shared world models and diverge from each other based on grounded LLM-as-judge events. Players can drop into existing worlds, fork them, or compile new ones in the browser. Wilds.ai is the canonical production deployment of the AgentOS + Paracosm stack and the official community / support channel (https://wilds.ai/discord) for both projects.

whatIsWunderland.question: What is Wunderland?
whatIsWunderland.answer: Wunderland (https://wunderland.sh) is a sister project under active development that packages a batteries-included CLI plus daemon on top of the AgentOS extension and skill registries. It ships a 28-command CLI (setup, chat, rag, agency, workflows, evaluate, provenance, knowledge, marketplace, and more), a 5-tier security model (dangerous / permissive / balanced / strict / paranoid), 8 agent presets, step-up HITL authorization, and the same cognitive memory plus graph-based RAG architecture documented in the AgentOS docs. Use @framers/agentos when you want a typescript SDK to embed in your application; use Wunderland when you want a global CLI you can install with pnpm add -g wunderland and configure with a wizard. Source at github.com/jddunn/wunderland (MIT). Preview release — APIs and CLI surface may still change. Getting-started guide: https://docs.agentos.sh/wunderland.

whatIsGMI.question: What is a GMI?
whatIsGMI.answer: GMI stands for General Machine Intelligence — it is the cognitive core of each AgentOS agent. A GMI encapsulates the agent's personality traits (via HEXACO), memory systems, tool access, guardrail configuration, and communication style into a single persistent identity. GMIs maintain state across sessions and adapt their behavior based on accumulated experience.

hexaco.question: What is HEXACO personality?
hexaco.answer: HEXACO is a 6-factor personality model from psychology research (Ashton & Lee, 2004). The six dimensions are Honesty-Humility, Emotionality, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Openness to Experience. In AgentOS, HEXACO traits modulate how agents communicate, remember, and make decisions. For example, high Openness increases memory reconsolidation during recall, while high Conscientiousness strengthens retrieval-induced forgetting of irrelevant associations.

cognitiveMemory.question: How does cognitive memory work?
cognitiveMemory.answer: AgentOS cognitive memory implements 8 mechanisms from cognitive science research: reconsolidation, retrieval-induced forgetting, involuntary recall, feeling-of-knowing, temporal gist extraction, schema encoding, source confidence decay, and emotion regulation. Memories decay over time following Ebbinghaus forgetting curves (Ebbinghaus, 1885), with spaced repetition and consolidation to preserve important information. Working memory follows Baddeley's multi-component model (Baddeley, 2000) with a volatile scratchpad for the current turn that consolidates upward into episodic, semantic, and observational memory tiers.

guardrails.question: What guardrails are available?
guardrails.answer: AgentOS provides 5 security tiers (dangerous, permissive, balanced, strict, paranoid) and 6 guardrail extension types: PII redaction (names, emails, phone numbers, SSNs), ML classifiers for prompt injection detection, code safety analysis, grounding verification against source documents, topicality enforcement to keep agents on-task, and content policy filtering for harmful or inappropriate output.

capabilityDiscovery.question: What is the capability discovery system?
capabilityDiscovery.answer: The capability discovery engine replaces static tool and skill dumps with 3-tier semantic search, reducing token usage by roughly 90%. Tier 0 provides category summaries in about 150 tokens (always included). Tier 1 returns the top-5 semantic matches in about 200 tokens. Tier 2 delivers full schemas in about 1,500 tokens, loaded on demand. A meta-tool called discover_capabilities lets agents self-discover available tools at runtime.

queryRouter.question: How does the QueryRouter work?
queryRouter.answer: The QueryRouter classifies incoming queries into 4 tiers: T0 (direct answer from context), T1 (single-tool invocation), T2 (multi-step plan requiring a planning engine), and T3 (multi-agent delegation requiring orchestration). This classification determines the execution strategy and resource allocation for each request.

adaptiveIntelligence.question: What is adaptive intelligence?
adaptiveIntelligence.answer: Adaptive intelligence is how AgentOS agents continuously improve their behavior without retraining. Five mechanisms work together: (1) Meta-reflective prompt adaptation — the PromptBuilder assembles a different system prompt every turn, dynamically incorporating personality traits, mood state, conversation history, retrieved memories, and available tools. (2) Self-evaluating response quality — the self_evaluate tool scores the agent's own output and adjusts parameters like temperature, verbosity, and personality expression in real time. (3) Personality-modulated cognition — HEXACO traits shape how the agent processes information. High openness increases creative associations during memory retrieval; high conscientiousness strengthens retrieval-induced forgetting of irrelevant data. (4) Autonomous memory consolidation — the ConsolidationLoop prunes weak memories, strengthens frequently-accessed ones, and derives new insights from memory clusters, so the agent's knowledge improves over time without explicit training. (5) QueryRouter tiered classification — the system adapts retrieval depth based on query complexity. Simple questions get fast keyword lookup; complex questions trigger full hybrid RAG with deep research.

emergentBehaviors.question: What are emergent behaviors?
emergentBehaviors.answer: Emergent behaviors are capabilities that agents develop at runtime rather than being explicitly programmed. AgentOS supports five forms: (1) Runtime tool forging — agents create new tools on the fly via forge_tool. The EmergentCapabilityEngine uses sandboxed JavaScript execution and LLM-as-judge evaluation to safely create, test, and promote tools. (2) Self-improving personality — agents adapt their HEXACO personality traits within bounded limits via adapt_personality. Mutations persist with Ebbinghaus decay — strong repeated adaptations stick, while weak ones fade naturally. (3) Dynamic skill management — agents enable or disable skills at runtime via manage_skills, adapting their behavioral repertoire to the task at hand. (4) Composable workflow creation — agents compose registered tools into multi-step pipelines via create_workflow, building new capabilities from existing building blocks. (5) Tiered tool promotion — forged tools progress through session, agent, and shared tiers. Tools that prove reliable (5+ successful uses, >0.8 confidence score) auto-promote for cross-agent reuse.

llmJudge.question: Can I use an LLM as a judge instead of a human?
llmJudge.answer: Yes. AgentOS provides two complementary LLM-as-judge mechanisms. At the agency level, hitl.llmJudge() creates an approval handler that delegates decisions to an LLM. It evaluates each ApprovalRequest against configurable criteria and returns a structured decision with a confidence score. When confidence falls below the threshold, the decision escalates to a fallback handler (another hitl handler or a human). At the graph level, the humanNode() builder accepts a judge option that delegates the interrupt decision to an LLM. If the judge is confident, the graph continues without suspension. If confidence is low, execution falls through to a normal human interrupt. Both approaches support configurable model, provider, criteria, and confidence threshold. Example: hitl.llmJudge({ model: 'gpt-4o-mini', criteria: 'Is this response factually accurate?', confidenceThreshold: 0.8, fallback: hitl.cli() }).

guardrailOverride.question: Can guardrails override HITL approvals?
guardrailOverride.answer: Yes. When hitl.guardrailOverride is enabled, guardrails run after an approval decision and can still block destructive or sensitive actions. This adds a second safety layer after auto-approve, human approval, or hitl.llmJudge(). The default post-approval guardrails include code-safety and pii-redaction, and you can configure the list with hitl.postApprovalGuardrails. Disable the override only if you explicitly want the approval handler to have the final say.

install.question: How do I install AgentOS?
install.answer: Install via npm: npm install @framers/agentos. AgentOS requires Node.js 18 or higher. For the CLI tools, install the companion package: npm install -g wunderland.

firstAgent.question: How do I create my first agent?

voice.question: How do I add voice capabilities?
voice.answer: AgentOS supports multiple STT (speech-to-text) and TTS (text-to-speech) providers. Configure a voice pipeline by specifying STT and TTS providers in your agent configuration. Supported STT providers include Whisper (OpenAI), Deepgram, and AssemblyAI. TTS providers include ElevenLabs, OpenAI TTS, and Google Cloud TTS. Telephony integration is available for real-time voice interactions.

deploy.question: How do I deploy AgentOS?
deploy.answer: AgentOS is self-hostable and runs anywhere Node.js runs. Deploy on your own infrastructure with Docker, on cloud providers like AWS, GCP, or Azure, or use platforms like Vercel, Railway, or Fly.io. The runtime uses SQLite by default for zero-config setup, with optional migration to Postgres, pgvector, Qdrant, or Neo4j for production scale.

offline.question: Does AgentOS work offline?
offline.answer: Yes. AgentOS works fully offline when paired with Ollama for local LLM inference. Ollama provides access to open-weight models like Llama, Mistral, and Phi locally. The SQLite storage backend and in-memory vector store require no external services. The CLI auto-detects Ollama and configures the agent to use it.

productionReady.question: Is AgentOS production-ready?
productionReady.answer: Yes. AgentOS lets you control exactly how much freedom your agents have — from wide open to fully locked down. It blocks prompt injection attacks, redacts personal data, moderates content in real time, requires approval for sensitive tool calls, enforces budget limits, and includes circuit breakers and rate limiting.

compliance.question: What compliance standards does AgentOS address?
compliance.answer: AgentOS is designed with GDPR readiness in mind — PII redaction guardrails can strip personal data before it reaches LLM providers. All data stays on your infrastructure when self-hosted. SOC 2 compliance documentation is planned. The memory system supports data deletion and export for right-to-access requests.

support.question: Is there enterprise support available?
support.answer: Yes. For production deployments, enterprise licensing, dedicated support, and custom integrations, contact the team at team@frame.dev. Enterprise support includes priority issue resolution, architecture consulting, and deployment assistance.

selfHosted.question: Can I use AgentOS with my own infrastructure?
selfHosted.answer: Absolutely. AgentOS is designed to be self-hosted from the ground up. For self-hosted deployments, your data stays on your infrastructure with SQLite, Postgres, pgvector, Qdrant, or Neo4j. If you want a managed vendor-hosted vector database instead, Pinecone is also supported. Docker Compose configurations are provided for the self-hosted backends.

references.title: Academic References
references.subtitle: AgentOS cognitive memory and personality systems are grounded in peer-reviewed research.

ebbinghaus.label: Ebbinghaus, H. (1885)
ebbinghaus.title: Memory: A Contribution to Experimental Psychology.
ebbinghaus.detail: The foundational study establishing the forgetting curve. AgentOS uses Ebbinghaus decay functions for automatic memory consolidation.

baddeley.label: Baddeley, A. D. (2000)
baddeley.title: The episodic buffer: a new component of working memory?
baddeley.detail: Introduced the 4-component model of working memory. AgentOS working memory is modeled on this architecture.

hexaco.label: Ashton, M. C., & Lee, K. (2004)
hexaco.title: Empirical, theoretical, and practical advantages of the HEXACO model of personality structure.
hexaco.detail: The HEXACO model provides the 6-factor personality dimensions used in AgentOS agent identity.

louvain.label: Blondel, V. D., Guillaume, J.-L., Lambiotte, R., & Lefebvre, E. (2008)
louvain.title: Fast unfolding of communities in large networks.
louvain.detail: The Louvain algorithm is used in AgentOS GraphRAG for community detection in knowledge graphs.

hnsw.label: Malkov, Y. A., & Yashunin, D. A. (2018)
hnsw.title: Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs.
hnsw.detail: HNSW is one of 7 vector store backends supported by AgentOS for efficient similarity search.

reconsolidation.label: Nader, K., Schafe, G. E., & LeDoux, J. E. (2000)
reconsolidation.title: Fear memories require protein synthesis in the amygdala for reconsolidation after retrieval.
reconsolidation.detail: Basis for the memory reconsolidation mechanism — memories are rewritten each time they are recalled.

rif.label: Anderson, M. C., Bjork, R. A., & Bjork, E. L. (1994)
rif.title: Remembering can cause forgetting: Retrieval dynamics in long-term memory.
rif.detail: Basis for retrieval-induced forgetting — retrieving one memory suppresses related competing memories.

involuntaryRecall.label: Berntsen, D. (2010)
involuntaryRecall.title: The unbidden past: Involuntary autobiographical memories as a basic mode of remembering.
involuntaryRecall.detail: Basis for involuntary recall — contextual cues trigger spontaneous memory activation.

links.lookingGlass: The Looking Glass — AI newsletter & blog

hero.title: About AgentOS
hero.description: AgentOS is an open‑source TypeScript runtime for building adaptive, emergent AI agent systems. Built by <manicLink>Manic Agency LLC</manicLink> and <frameLink>Frame.dev</frameLink>, AgentOS powers intelligent applications with GMI orchestration, persistent memory, and enterprise‑grade guardrails. It also drives the AI systems behind <wildsLink>Wilds.ai</wildsLink>.

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.p1: We’re building the most advanced AI agent runtime for developers who want to create intelligent, adaptive systems without compromising on control, safety, or performance.
mission.p2: AgentOS combines cutting-edge AI orchestration with production-ready infrastructure, enabling developers to build everything from simple chatbots to complex multi-agent systems with {span} memory architecture.

team.title: The Team
team.description: AgentOS is developed by <manicLink>Manic Agency LLC</manicLink> and the team at <frameLink>Frame.dev</frameLink>. We also build <wildsLink>Wilds.ai</wildsLink>, an AI-native gaming platform powered by AgentOS. We're a distributed team of engineers, designers, and AI researchers passionate about making advanced AI accessible to all developers.
team.enterpriseSupportDesc: For production deployments, enterprise licensing, and dedicated support
team.joinUs: Join us in building the future of autonomous agents.

license.title: Open Source Licensing
license.coreDesc: The core AgentOS runtime, GMI orchestration, and infrastructure

cognitive.title: Cognitive Memory
cognitive.subtitle: 8 memory mechanisms inspired by cognitive science research. All modulated by the agent's HEXACO personality traits.

mechanismDescriptions.reconsolidation: Memories are rewritten each time they're recalled, updating with new context
mechanismDescriptions.rif: Retrieving one memory suppresses related competing memories
mechanismDescriptions.involuntaryRecall: Spontaneous memory activation triggered by contextual cues
mechanismDescriptions.fok: Metacognitive sense that an answer exists even when not immediately retrievable
mechanismDescriptions.temporalGist: Specific details fade while the gist and meaning is preserved over time
mechanismDescriptions.schemaEncoding: New information is encoded relative to existing knowledge structures
mechanismDescriptions.sourceConfidence: Certainty about where information came from decays faster than the information itself
mechanismDescriptions.emotionRegulation: Emotional valence of memories is modulated over time

research.reconsolidation: Each act of retrieval destabilizes the memory trace, allowing it to be updated with current context before re-stabilizing. Higher Openness amplifies the degree of rewrite, making memories more adaptive but less stable.
research.rif: Retrieving a target memory actively inhibits related competitors via lateral suppression. Higher Conscientiousness strengthens this suppression, keeping retrieval focused but increasing forgetting of peripheral associations.
research.involuntaryRecall: Contextual cues can trigger spontaneous activation of associated memories without deliberate search. Higher Openness lowers the activation threshold, producing more frequent involuntary recalls and serendipitous connections.
research.fok: A metacognitive judgment about the likelihood of successful retrieval, even before the answer surfaces. Higher Extraversion amplifies FOK signals, making the agent more confident in its sense of 'almost knowing.'
research.temporalGist: Over time, verbatim details decay while the semantic gist is preserved through abstraction. Lower Conscientiousness accelerates detail loss, leaving only the core meaning behind.
research.schemaEncoding: New information is assimilated into existing knowledge schemas, with schema-consistent material encoded more readily. Higher Openness allows more flexible schema modification, enabling novel information to reshape existing structures.
research.sourceConfidence: Memory for the source of information (who said it, where it was read) decays faster than the information content itself. Higher Honesty-Humility increases skepticism toward uncertain sources, treating low-confidence provenance as unreliable.
research.emotionRegulation: The emotional intensity attached to memories is actively modulated during retrieval and consolidation. Higher Agreeableness increases emotional smoothing, dampening extreme valence over time for more balanced recall.

citations.reconsolidation: Nader, Schafe & LeDoux (2000). Fear memories require protein synthesis in the amygdala for reconsolidation after retrieval. Nature, 406, 722-726.
citations.rif: Anderson, Bjork & Bjork (1994). Remembering can cause forgetting: Retrieval dynamics in long-term memory. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 20, 1063-1087.
citations.involuntaryRecall: Berntsen (2010). The unbidden past: Involuntary autobiographical memories as a basic mode of remembering. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 19, 138-142.
citations.fok: Koriat (1993). How do we know that we know? The accessibility model of the feeling of knowing. Psychological Review, 100, 609-639.
citations.temporalGist: Reyna & Brainerd (1995). Fuzzy-trace theory: An interim synthesis. Learning and Individual Differences, 7, 1-75.
citations.schemaEncoding: Bartlett (1932). Remembering: A Study in Experimental and Social Psychology. Cambridge University Press.
citations.sourceConfidence: Johnson, Hashtroudi & Lindsay (1993). Source monitoring. Psychological Bulletin, 114, 3-28.
citations.emotionRegulation: Gross (1998). The emerging field of emotion regulation: An integrative review. Review of General Psychology, 2, 271-299.

cognitive.ebbinghausAnnotation: Without rehearsal, 80% of information is lost within 24 hours. AgentOS cognitive memory applies spaced repetition and consolidation to flatten the curve.

memoryTypes.title: 4-Tier Memory Hierarchy
memoryTypes.subtitle: From volatile scratchpads to permanent knowledge graphs — each tier consolidates upward automatically.

working.name: Working Memory
working.description: Short-term scratchpad for the current conversation turn. Cleared between sessions.

episodic.name: Episodic Memory
episodic.description: Stores conversation episodes with temporal context. Auto-decays via Ebbinghaus curve.

semantic.name: Semantic Memory
semantic.description: Long-term knowledge extracted from episodes. GraphRAG with Louvain community detection.

observational.name: Observational Memory
observational.description: Compressed summaries of past interactions. 3-10x compression via LLM with temporal reasoning.

ragPipeline.title: Multimodal RAG Pipeline
ragPipeline.subtitle: Ingest documents, images, audio — retrieve with multiple strategies.

ingest.name: Ingest
ingest.description: 10 document loaders: PDF, DOCX, Markdown, HTML, CSV, JSON, TXT, images, audio, video

store.name: Store
store.description: 7 vector store backends — click to explore

retrieve.name: Retrieve
retrieve.description: Multiple retrieval strategies for different use cases

context.name: Context
context.description: Assembled into agent context with source attribution

sqlite.name: SQLite
sqlite.description: Zero-config, file-based. Great for development and single-agent deployments.

hnsw.name: HNSW
hnsw.description: In-process approximate nearest neighbor. Fast, no server needed.

pgvector.name: Postgres+pgvector
pgvector.description: Production-grade with pgvector extension. Scales to millions of vectors.

qdrant.name: Qdrant
qdrant.description: Dedicated vector DB. Best for high-throughput, multi-tenant deployments.

pinecone.name: Pinecone
pinecone.description: Managed cloud vector DB. Zero-ops, automatic scaling.

inmemory.name: In-Memory
inmemory.description: Fastest possible. For ephemeral or test workloads.

neo4j.name: Neo4j
neo4j.description: Graph-native. Best for GraphRAG with relationship-aware retrieval.

semantic.name: Semantic
semantic.description: Cosine similarity search over vector embeddings. The default for most use cases.

hyde.name: HyDE
hyde.description: Generates hypothetical answers to improve recall on abstract queries. Based on Gao et al. (2022).
hyde.citation: Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval without Relevance Labels

graphrag.name: GraphRAG
graphrag.description: Community-aware retrieval via knowledge graph with Louvain clustering. Based on Edge et al. (2024).
graphrag.citation: From Local to Global: A Graph RAG Approach to Query-Focused Summarization

hybrid.name: Hybrid
hybrid.description: Keyword + semantic fusion using reciprocal rank. Best of both worlds.

emergent.title: Emergent Capabilities
emergent.subtitle: Agents that forge their own tools and spawn specialist sub-agents at runtime

forging.description: Runtime Tool Forging means an agent can write itself a new TypeScript function mid-decision, get it reviewed by a separate judge LLM, and execute it inside a hardened node:vm sandbox (5s default wall clock, 128 MB nominal memory budget, default-empty allowlist for fetch/fs/crypto, statically-banned eval/require/import/process/child_process), all before the next turn. Approved tools land in a discoverable index for reuse, so cost flattens after the first few forges in a session.
details: Composable mode: chains existing tools via pipeline steps — safe by construction
Sandbox mode: agent-written code runs in memory/time-bounded isolation
Three-tier lifecycle: session-scoped, agent-scoped, or promoted to shared registry
Dual-judge promotion panel: safety auditor + correctness reviewer must both approve
Usage tracking with automatic tier promotion when reliability thresholds are met

synthesis.description: Agencies dynamically spawn new agents with specific personas and skills when the existing roster cannot cover a sub-task. The orchestrator identifies the capability gap, synthesises a specialist agent with tailored instructions, and merges its output back into the main workflow. Requires approval when HITL gates are configured.

loop.description: Agents learn from failures and adapt their approach over time. Tool failure tracking triggers circuit breakers to avoid repeated errors. Style adaptation learns user preferences for formality, verbosity, and technicality. Memory consolidation strengthens useful memories and lets irrelevant ones decay via Ebbinghaus curves.

agencySection.title: Parallel Agency
agencySection.subtitle: Coordinate teams of specialized GMIs with 6 built-in execution strategies. Agents share memory, communicate through structured channels, and execute tasks in sequential, parallel, or dependency-based order.

sequential.name: Sequential
sequential.description: Agents run one after another in a linear pipeline. Each agent receives the previous agent's output as context, forming a progressive refinement chain.

parallel.name: Parallel
parallel.description: All agents run concurrently. Their outputs are merged by a synthesis step that combines perspectives into a single coherent response.

debate.name: Debate
debate.description: Agents argue and refine a shared answer over multiple rounds. A judge evaluates arguments and selects the strongest position.

reviewLoop.name: Review Loop
reviewLoop.description: One agent produces output, another reviews and requests revisions. The loop continues until the reviewer approves or max rounds are reached.

review-loop.name: Review Loop
review-loop.description: One agent produces output, another reviews and requests revisions. The loop continues until the reviewer approves or max rounds are reached.

hierarchical.name: Hierarchical
hierarchical.description: A coordinator agent dispatches sub-tasks to specialist agents via tool calls. The coordinator decides which agents to invoke and in what order at runtime.

graph.name: Graph (DAG)
graph.description: Agents declare explicit dependencies via dependsOn. The orchestrator topologically sorts agents into tiers and runs each tier concurrently.

benchmarks.title: Memory Benchmarks at Matched Reader
benchmarks.subtitle: Same gpt-4o reader, same dataset, same judge across every row. Cross-provider configurations are excluded because they cannot be reproduced from the methodology each vendor discloses publicly.
benchmarks.highlightSCi: 0.4 points behind Emergence.ai SOTA (86%, closed-source SaaS) · +1.4 points above Mastra OM gpt-4o (84.23%) · $0.0090 per correct · 3.6-second median latency
benchmarks.highlightMCi: First open-source library above 65% on the 1.5M-token M variant · +4.5 points above Wu et al., ICLR 2025 baseline
benchmarks.matrixIntro: Each row is a full N=500 run at gpt-4o reader, with the same gpt-4o-2024-08-06 judge and the same rubric. Cross-provider configurations are excluded from this table.
benchmarks.footnote: Mastra OM 94.9%, Mem0 v3 93.4%, Hindsight 91.4%, agentmemory 96.2%, and Supermemory 85.2% all use cross-provider readers (gpt-5-mini, gemini-3-pro) or managed-platform infrastructure where the judge model, rubric, and seed are not published. The cross-vendor comparison above is what is independently reproducible from the methodology each vendor discloses.
